title: Code Detectives: Debug & Improve!
subtitle: find the bug, fix the logic, level up!
overview: Students will learn the systematic art of troubleshooting and optimization by tracking down syntax errors, fixing logical bugs in broken code blocks, and implementing performance improvements.

images._note: Add photos from the actual session — red compilation error screens, code comparison charts, whiteboard debugging workflows.

keyTopics: The Bug Origin Story — Grace Hopper's Moth | How a real moth stuck in a 1947 relay station named a software movement
Syntax Errors — The Spelling Bee | Broken rules, unclosed brackets, and missing semicolons that make compilers crash
Logical Bugs — The Misdirected Map | Code that compiles and runs smoothly, but achieves the completely wrong real-world output
Print Debugging — The Flashlight Trick | Dropping visual status alerts throughout your logic line to trace variable value shifts
Refactoring — Room Organization | Cleaning up and streamlining repetitive lines without altering the program's primary goal
Code Comments — Future Letters | Writing clear documentation tags so your future self can understand what your build achieves

resources.title: Code Hunt — Structural Problem Solving Exercises

activity.title: The Grand Bug Hunt Challenge
parts: Workstation computers loaded with pre-broken software project templates
Red and Green debugging checklist tracker cards
A software project script containing 3 hidden syntax traps and 2 logic bugs
Reference sheet containing standard programming rules syntax mappings
steps: Open up the buggy challenge file in your IDE—try compiling it immediately and watch the errors pile up!
Locate the first error line code row flagged by the compiler console panel.
Hunt down and correct the missing punctuation markers (unmatched braces, missing semicolons, or misspelled variables).
Once compilation succeeds, execute the software and observe why the system behaves incorrectly (e.g., a green LED turns on instead of a red alarm).
Insert temporary print status code blocks right above the conditional logic checks to reveal active variables values on screen.
Fix the mathematical sign error or inverted logic gates causing the structural malfunction.
Consolidate repetitive code clusters into streamlined execution steps, leaving descriptive comments over your fixes.
Challenge: Swap files with a nearby peer and test if your updated scripts can survive edge-case inputs without glitching!

quiz.q: What is the critical difference between a SYNTAX error and a LOGICAL bug?
options: Syntax errors happen in hardware; logical bugs happen inside cloud setups
Syntax errors break code punctuation rules so it won't run; logical bugs compile fine but output wrong results
Syntax errors slow down execution speeds; logical bugs instantly delete files from hard drives
There is no difference; both problems are fixed by turning off the wall switch
quiz.explanation: Syntax errors are grammar violations that prevent your script from turning into an executable file. Logical bugs mean your script structure runs perfectly but follows a broken plan (like instructing a robot to turn left when it should turn right).

quiz.q: Why do software developers drop text print lines like 'Serial.println(variable)' throughout their workspace scripts when troubleshooting?
options: To make the final execution display screen colorful and interesting to watch
To spy inside variable changes real-time, verifying exactly where computational logic gets off-track
To clear out cache files stored inside processing units automatically
To add automated comments that explain what lines achieve
quiz.explanation: Printing variables out is like using a flashlight inside your code! It shows you the exact value of a sensor or variable at any given step, revealing where calculations break.

quiz.q: What does the process of 'Refactoring' code imply?
options: Completely erasing an entire project and rewriting it from absolute scratch
Changing the core behavior of your program so it does something completely different
Cleaning up and organizing code structure to make it cleaner and more efficient without altering how it works
Encrypting text files with secure passcode layers so others cannot open them
quiz.explanation: Refactoring is like tidying your bedroom! You aren't changing what house you live in; you're just organizing layout arrays so things run smoothly, efficiently, and are easy to read.

quiz.q: If your program requires a sensor threshold check to trigger only when temperature values exceed 30 degrees, which line represents a fixed and corrected setup?
options: if (temperature < 30) { triggerAlarm(); }
if (temperature == 30) { triggerAlarm(); }
if (temperature > 30) { triggerAlarm(); }
if (temperature = 30) { triggerAlarm(); }
quiz.explanation: The 'greater than' sign (>) correctly flags conditions rising above 30. Option 4 uses a single (=) which mistakenly reassigns numbers instead of comparing them!

quiz.q: What is considered a best practice when writing code comments in a complex workspace setup?
options: Write a comment on every single line describing what a basic bracket does
Leave vague inside joke notes that only you understand
Write concise notes explaining the 'why' behind complex logic branches so others can safely update it later
Avoid using comments entirely because they slow down execution performance on microcontrollers
quiz.explanation: Comments don't affect computer speed at all—the compiler ignores them. They exist as human signposts to help teammates (and your future self) understand complex engineering blocks easily.
